title: Sorting Algorithms Overview
summary: Comparison of common sorting algorithms including their time and space complexities, stability, and best use cases.
key_points: Bubble Sort: O(n²) average, stable, in-place — good for educational purposes
Merge Sort: O(n log n) guaranteed, stable, requires O(n) extra space
Quick Sort: O(n log n) average, O(n²) worst case, in-place but unstable
Heap Sort: O(n log n) guaranteed, in-place but unstable
Counting Sort: O(n+k) for integers with limited range — not comparison-based

title: Dynamic Programming Techniques
summary: Core dynamic programming strategies including memoization vs tabulation, identifying overlapping subproblems, and optimal substructure.
key_points: Memoization (top-down): cache results of recursive calls
Tabulation (bottom-up): iteratively fill a table from base cases
Key indicators: overlapping subproblems + optimal substructure
Classic problems: Fibonacci, Knapsack, LCS, Matrix Chain Multiplication
Always define the state and transition before coding

title: Graph Algorithms & Traversals
summary: Fundamental graph traversal and shortest path algorithms used frequently in exams.
key_points: BFS: Level-order traversal, uses queue, O(V+E), finds shortest path in unweighted graphs
DFS: Depth-first, uses stack/recursion, O(V+E), used for cycle detection and topological sort
Dijkstra: Shortest path for non-negative weights, uses priority queue, O((V+E) log V)
Bellman-Ford: Handles negative weights, O(VE), detects negative cycles
Kruskal/Prim: Minimum spanning tree algorithms

title: Process Scheduling Algorithms
summary: Overview of CPU scheduling algorithms, their characteristics, and trade-offs in operating system design.
key_points: FCFS: Simple, non-preemptive, suffers from convoy effect
SJF: Optimal average wait time, but requires knowing burst time in advance
Round Robin: Preemptive, fair, performance depends on time quantum
Priority Scheduling: Can cause starvation — solved by aging
Multilevel Queue: Different queues for different process types

title: Memory Management & Paging
summary: How operating systems manage memory allocation, paging, and virtual memory concepts.
key_points: Paging: Divides memory into fixed-size pages, eliminates external fragmentation
Page Table: Maps virtual pages to physical frames
TLB (Translation Lookaside Buffer): Cache for page table entries — speeds up address translation
Page Replacement: FIFO, LRU, Optimal — each with different trade-offs
Thrashing: Excessive paging that severely degrades performance

title: Deadlock Conditions & Prevention
summary: Understanding deadlock, its necessary conditions, and strategies for handling it.
key_points: Four conditions (all must hold): Mutual Exclusion, Hold & Wait, No Preemption, Circular Wait
Prevention: Negate one of the four conditions
Avoidance: Banker's Algorithm — check safe state before allocation
Detection: Resource Allocation Graph (RAG) — look for cycles
Recovery: Process termination or resource preemption

title: Normalization Forms
summary: Database normalization process from 1NF to BCNF with practical examples and guidelines.
key_points: 1NF: Atomic values, no repeating groups
2NF: 1NF + no partial dependencies (all non-key attributes depend on entire primary key)
3NF: 2NF + no transitive dependencies
BCNF: Every determinant is a candidate key — stricter than 3NF
Denormalization: Sometimes used for performance at the cost of redundancy

title: SQL Joins & Subqueries
summary: Essential SQL join types and subquery patterns commonly tested in database exams.
key_points: INNER JOIN: Returns matching rows from both tables
LEFT JOIN: All rows from left table + matched rows from right
RIGHT JOIN: All rows from right table + matched rows from left
FULL OUTER JOIN: All rows from both tables, NULL for non-matches
Correlated Subquery: References outer query — executes once per outer row

title: Transaction Management & ACID
summary: How databases ensure reliable transactions through ACID properties and concurrency control.
key_points: Atomicity: Transaction is all-or-nothing
Consistency: Database moves from one valid state to another
Isolation: Concurrent transactions don't interfere (levels: Read Uncommitted → Serializable)
Durability: Committed changes survive system failures
Concurrency control: Locks (2PL), timestamps, MVCC

title: OSI Model Layers
summary: Complete breakdown of the 7-layer OSI networking model with protocols and functions at each layer.
key_points: Layer 7 (Application): HTTP, FTP, SMTP, DNS — user-facing services
Layer 4 (Transport): TCP (reliable) vs UDP (fast) — port-based multiplexing
Layer 3 (Network): IP addressing, routing (OSPF, BGP), packet forwarding
Layer 2 (Data Link): MAC addressing, Ethernet, switches, error detection
Layer 1 (Physical): Bits on wire, cables, hubs, signal encoding

title: TCP/IP Protocol Suite
summary: Deep dive into TCP/IP protocols, the three-way handshake, and reliable data transfer mechanisms.
key_points: Three-way handshake: SYN → SYN-ACK → ACK establishes connection
Flow Control: Sliding window protocol prevents receiver buffer overflow
Congestion Control: Slow start, congestion avoidance, fast retransmit
IP Addressing: IPv4 (32-bit) vs IPv6 (128-bit), CIDR notation
NAT: Translates private IPs to public IPs for internet access

title: Network Security Fundamentals
summary: Core network security concepts including encryption, authentication, and common attacks.
key_points: Symmetric encryption: Same key for encrypt/decrypt (AES, DES) — fast
Asymmetric encryption: Public/private key pairs (RSA) — slower but more secure
TLS/SSL: Encrypts HTTP traffic, uses certificates for authentication
Common attacks: Man-in-the-middle, DDoS, SQL injection, XSS
Firewalls: Packet filtering, stateful inspection, application-layer gateway
